aesthetic: The Annotated Margin — the philosophical manuscript page where two traditions read one another
anchor: Husserl's personal copy of James's 'Principles of Psychology', preserved at the Husserl Archives in Leuven, marked in Gabelsberger stenographic shorthand (selective heavy marking in Chs. 9 and 15; five diagonal strokes along Hodgson's footnote) — crossed with the phenomenological epoche bracket and Dewey's reflex-arc circuit redrawn as a closed loop. The page is set in two inks: a Prussian violet-indigo for the descriptive gaze inward (phenomenology) and an iron-gall oxblood for the gaze outward to consequences (pragmatism).
rationale: The corpus's load-bearing evidence is literally a marginated manuscript page: one tradition's hand annotating the other's printed text. Its structural metaphor is a bipolar one — experience described from within (phenomenology) and meaning clarified by its practical effects (pragmatism) — two movements that began at the same moment on two continents, read one another, and converged. So the aesthetic is the manuscript page itself: warm laid paper, two inks (indigo + oxblood) for the two gazes, the epoche bracket and the marginal stroke as ornament, sharp printed-page corners. Lucid, two-handed, calibrated — descriptive, never persuasive. Green carries the empirical-psychology register, aubergine the theological register, ochre the late convergence.

fonts.display: 'Palatino Linotype', Palatino, 'Book Antiqua', 'URW Palladio L', Georgia, serif
